1 BAY STREET | SAG HARBOR LONG WHARF | INSPIRED BY POWER BY KANYE WEST

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS

[Verse 1]

[22] "Sag Harbor Long Wharf, trade and commerce benefit mankind" → Directly from: "Trade and commerce are in general a benefit to mankind, and in particular to the inhabitants of this town." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 14.

[22] "Seventeen-seventy, thirty rods northerly, pier by design" → Drawn from: "February 12th, 1770, the Town of East-Hampton, by their trustees, made a grant of liberty to build at Sag-Harbor a wharf. 'Beginning at Southampton on East Patent line where Southampton grant for said wharf ends, and to run northerly thirty rods, said wharf to contain forty rights, reckoned at twenty pounds a right.'" Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 14.

[22] "Forty rights at twenty pounds, East Hampton made the grant" → Drawn from: "said wharf to contain forty rights, reckoned at twenty pounds a right." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 14.

[3] "Cooper shops and try houses, whale oil you can't recant" → Drawn from: "Here is the first mention of a cooper's shop at Sag Harbor. Demands for casks to hold whale oil, were in years after this and up to 1870, to keep many cooper shops along the Sag Harbor waterfront busy." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 138.

[1] "Eighty dwelling houses, five thousand tons of ships enrolled" → Directly from: "There were 80 dwelling houses at Sag Harbor in 1810, and the port registered 5,000 tons of vessels." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 209.

[12] "Port of entry, Dering collecting customs, Washington signed the scroll" → Drawn from: "Sag Harbor had its federal custom house, in 1788, and Henry P. Dering received his appointment as collector of customs from Congress in 1790, signed by President George Washington." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 108.

[Chorus]

[30] "Long Wharf stretching out, a thousand feet across the bay" → Drawn from: "making the Long Wharf and what was called the State Wharf (part of the extension) about 1,100 feet long from fill in of land, and about 1,000 feet long as a wharf or pier structure or bridge over the water." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 127.

[34] "Whale ships coming home, flag flying from the mill that day" → Drawn from: "the Beebee windmill, from which was flown an American flag upon sight of a homeward bound ship, in Gardiner's Bay." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 165.

[46] "Deep water harbor, forest of tall masts fence the sides" → Drawn from: "Its natural, deep-water harbor" and "a forest of tall masts fenced the sides of the wharf." Eames. (2015) Oh That's Another Story, p. 36.

[45] "Sag Harbor rise again — we built this port on tides" → Inspired by: "a majority of the residents cleared away the rubble and started from scratch again. They were a strong and hardy bunch, determined to face the future no matter what it should bring." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 142.

[Verse 2]

[12] "Brig Lucy, Brazil Banks, seventeen eighty-five the run" → Directly from: "sending the brig 'Lucy' on a whaling voyage to Brazil Banks, in 1785, they were successful." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 108.

[12] "Huntting and Howell sent her out, the whaling had begun" → Drawn from: "Mostly credit has been given to Col. Benjamin Huntting and Capt. Stephen Howell, of Southampton, for reestablishing shipping and ship building enterprises at Sag Harbor." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 108.

[27] "Three thousand barrels oil, Pacific to Kamschatka cold" → Drawn from: "We took about 3500 barrels of whale oil and 150 barrels of sperm" and "from there across to the coast of Kamschatka." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 99.

[27] "Henry Lee round the Horn, three years before we rolled back home" → Drawn from: "came back to Long Island by way of Cape Horn after a three year voyage." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 99.

[20] "Nantucket men taught offshore whaling, natives knew the shore" → Drawn from: "Nantucket families and Sag Harbor families are related by marriage, and that Nantucket men really taught Sag Harbor men what they knew about 'off shore' whaling. The natives of East Hampton and Southampton were proficient in 'shore whaling,' a quite different enterprise." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 115.

[48] "Mehitable to the Indies, profits hundred thirty-five and more" → Drawn from: "There is information of adjustment of a voyage made by the sloop 'Mehitable,' Clay, master, in 1771—a West India voyage—with profits of 135 pounds sterling." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 8.

[Bridge]

[43] "Fire of forty-five burned ninety-five buildings to the ground" → Drawn from: "we are not much out of the way when we assert that there were somewhat in the neighborhood of ninety-five dwelling houses, stores, warehouses, tradesmen's shops, etc., destroyed." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 26.

[45] "Fire of seventy-seven, Minnehaha pumping, freezing sound" → Drawn from: "the department arrived at the scene and stationed the 'Minnehaha' wagon near the foot of Main Street where a public well was located" and "the boys backed the old 'Minnehaha' engine into the bay at the slip west of Huntting's block and stood in freezing water knee deep pumping for dear life." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 142.

[45] "But they cleared the rubble, started over, scratch again" → Directly from: "a majority of the residents cleared away the rubble and started from scratch again." Zaykowski. (1991) Sag Harbor: The Story of an American Beauty, p. 142.

[40] "Sandy streets unpaved but hardy people never bend" → Drawn from: "The streets are not paved, but very sandy, which in dry weather renders them very unpleasant to travel." Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 207.

[Outro]

[21] "From Wegwagonuck, the place at the end of the hill" → Directly from: "The Indians called the locality of Sag Harbor, Wegwagonuck, 'the place at the end of the hill.'" Sleight. (1997) Sag Harbor in Earlier Days, p. 10.

[22] "To the farthest oceans — Sag Harbor, power, will" → Drawn from: "more than four score years ago had conducted an enterprise that extended in vastness to the farthest oceans." Hedges. (1896) Early Sag Harbor, An Address Delivered Before the Sag-Harbor Historical Society, p. 14.
